one_line_takeaway: Corrective unlearning (removing a poison/bias from a trained model given only a small identified subset of the bad data) is a distinct problem from privacy unlearning where retrain-from-scratch is no longer a valid gold standard, and existing methods mostly fail: a small unidentified poison remnant sustains a backdoor, and only SSD partially succeeds (poison-only, at the cost of utility).
key_finding: No benchmarked method (including the RewoD gold standard) succeeds on BOTH manipulations simultaneously when 80% or less of the manipulated data is identified. For poisoning, RewoD shows essentially no corrective improvement until more than 80% of the poison is identified, even though only 1% (500 samples) of the training set was poisoned; the small unidentified remainder keeps the backdoor alive, so retrain-from-scratch is NOT a sufficient gold standard here. The lone encouraging result is SSD, which removes the BadNet poison's effect with just ~10% of the poison identified, demonstrating that corrective unlearning at alpha < 1 is possible. However SSD fails completely on Interclass Confusion (no improvement over the original model) and it significantly degrades utility (retain accuracy). A mechanistic probe explains the split: poisoning concentrates influence in a few outlier parameters (importance ratios up to ~800) that SSD can dampen, whereas IC spreads influence across parameters (max importance ratio < 200). On the binary PCam medical dataset, Scrub also works well because moving away from the single wrong label directly implies the correct label.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes a new problem the authors call 'Corrective Machine Unlearning': removing the influence of adversarially manipulated/corrupted training data from an already-trained model when only a small, representative subset of the corrupted samples has been identified. Its own contributions are (1) a formal setting and two-objective evaluation (corrective accuracy Acc_corr on the affected domain D_m, and retain accuracy Acc_retain on the rest), parameterized by alpha = |S_f|/|S_m|, the fraction of the manipulated set that developers have identified; (2) an argument that this setting has fundamentally different requirements from privacy-oriented unlearning (different goal, different gold standard, no privacy constraint) so that retrain-from-scratch on the retained data ceases to be a valid gold standard; and (3) an empirical benchmark showing that current state-of-the-art unlearning methods largely fail in this setting. The authors explicitly note they propose neither a new method nor a new manipulation; the novelty is the unifying problem formulation and the benchmark finding. This must not be confused with the cited methods (SSD, Scrub, CF, BadT, BadNets, Interclass Confusion) which are all prior work they benchmark or reuse.
threat_model: An adversary manipulates a small fraction of web-scraped/outsourced training data (features and/or labels) to inject a harmful property into the trained model: either a BadNet-style backdoor (a trigger pattern that forces misclassification to a target class) or systematic label confusion between two classes (bias). The manipulation TYPE is unknown to the model developer a priori, so a corrective method must work across manipulation types. Post-training, the developer detects only a small, uniformly-sampled representative subset S_f of the full manipulated set S_m (e.g. via rigorous monitoring of a sub-sampled slice of data), and cannot afford to discard the trained model. The defender's goal is to cheaply 'correct' the model's behavior on the affected domain D_m using only S_f, without full retraining. Adaptive adversaries who craft manipulations against the unlearning procedure are explicitly out of scope (named as future work).
limitations: Stated by the authors: only two, deliberately simple, non-adaptive manipulations are studied (they did not add more complex ones precisely because even these break existing methods); no adaptive adversary that knows the unlearning procedure is evaluated; synthetic-sample generation attacks are not covered; the scope is supervised image classification only (self-supervised/language settings noted as relevant but untested); and the setting assumes no false positives (no clean sample mistakenly flagged as manipulated). Reviewer-noted: results are on CIFAR-10/CIFAR-100/PCam with only 3 seeds and mainly a single 1% manipulation rate in the main paper; the paper contributes a problem framing and negative benchmark result rather than a working corrective method, and even the one partial success (SSD) does not generalize.

datasets_used: PCam / PatchCamelyon binary histopathology medical imaging (WideResNet-28x10)
quantitative_results: Only 1% of training data manipulated in main experiments (= 500 samples on CIFAR); IC on CIFAR-10 needed 10% for clear effects; Appendix B goes as low as 0.2% poison with similar trends
RewoD (retrain-from-scratch gold standard) shows no corrective improvement on poisoning until more than 80% of the manipulated set is identified
SSD recovers corrective accuracy (Acc_corr) on BadNet poisoning with only ~10% of the manipulated samples identified
SSD fails completely on Interclass Confusion (no improvement over the original 'None' model)

SSD change in retain accuracy (utility) under poisoning: -8.94 +/- 1.02 (CIFAR-10) and -3.25 +/- 0.53 (CIFAR-100), versus original 'None' retain accuracy of 90.52 (CIFAR-10) and 73.79 (CIFAR-100); other methods stay near 0 change
Under Interclass Confusion, SSD change in retain accuracy: -1.63 +/- 0.95 (CIFAR-10) and -5.43 +/- 3.62 (CIFAR-100); original 'None' retain accuracy 92.36 / 74.35
SSD parameter importance-ratio histogram: poisoning produces outliers up to ~800; Interclass Confusion maximum is below 200
BadNet trigger whitens ~0.3% of pixels at bottom-right and relabels to class 0; poison data is 3072-dimensional with a 500-sample identified subset
alpha (identified fraction) swept 0.1 to 1.0 in 0.1 steps; metrics over 3 seeds; Table 1 retain-accuracy stats averaged over the 10 alpha values
baselines_compared: RewoD - Retrain without Deletion set (exact/gold-standard retrain-from-scratch on retained data)
CF - Catastrophic Forgetting (fine-tune all layers on retained data; Goel et al. 2023)
SSD - Selective Synaptic Dampening (Foster et al. 2023)
BadT - Knowledge Distillation from a Bad Teacher (Chundawat et al. 2023b)
Scrub - Scalable Remembering and Unlearning Bound (Kurmanji et al. 2023)
